metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_132", "source_md": "How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Spatial distribution (at  1km  resolution) of the  PM_{2.5} -attributable premature death in mainland China (a), BTH (b), YRD (c), FWP (d), and PRD region (e) in 2020.", "references": ["The number of premature deaths attributable to ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  decreased by  $\\sim 0.07$  million  $(5.00\\%)$ , from 1.35 million  $(95\\%$  Confidence Interval (CI): 1.06-1.78) to 1.28 million  $(95\\%$  CI: 0.97-1.73) in the APPCAP (2014 to 2017) (Fig. 6a and Fig. S9) and showed a continuous declining trend, decreasing to 1.12 million  $(95\\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) by 2020 (Fig. 4 and Fig. 6a). The number of attributable premature deaths in most areas  $(\\sim 99.5\\%)$  of China is less than four cases per  $1\\mathrm{km}^2$  in 2020 (Fig. 4). Large numbers of premature deaths", "were identified in dense populations and advanced industrial areas in the BTH, YRD, PRD and FWP regions (Fig. 4 and Table 1), despite the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in these regions decreased over time. The high-density premature death values are mostly found in metropolitan cities such as Beijing, Shanghai and Guangzhou. The largest number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths per unit area ( $1\\mathrm{km}$ ) occurred in the FWP region. The  $4.72\\%$  region of PRD had more than four cases of premature death per  $\\mathrm{km}^2$ , which is higher than other three priority control regions ( $1.76\\%$ ,  $2.47\\%$ , and  $0.99\\%$  in BTH, YRD, and FWP, respectively) and the national average ( $0.35\\%$ ), indicating that the high population density also exacerbates  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths.", "The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths was reduced in  $68.38\\%$  areas of mainland China over 2014 to 2020 (Fig. 5). The most effectively controlled region was BTH, where over  $74\\%$  of the area showed a decline in the number premature deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure. The FWP produced the smallest decrease amongst the four priority control regions, with the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths reducing in only  $26.95\\%$  of the area. Regarding the decrease number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, unlike the largest decrease in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration occurred in BTH region, the largest decrease in the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature death occurred in YRD region, with a decrease number of 0.037 million  $(17.00\\%)$  in 2020 compared to 2014 (Table 1), followed by the PRD (decrease  $16.45\\%$ ), BTH (decrease  $10.51\\%$ ) and FWP (decrease  $8.99\\%$ ) regions.", "108°0'0\"E  \nFig. 5. Spatial distribution of changes in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature death in mainland China (a), BTH (b), YRD (c), FWP (d), PRD region (e) between 2014 and 2020 (where the numbers of premature deaths in 2014 and 2020 are both zero this is represented by a white background).", "We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\\sim 27.05\\%$  of global ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\\%$  and  $26\\%$  decrease in the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "premature death", "mainland China", "2020", "BTH", "YRD", "FWP", "PRD"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows the spatial distribution of PM2.5-attributable premature deaths per 1 km² in mainland China, highlighting regions such as BTH, YRD, FWP, and PRD in 2020.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates the concentration of PM2.5-attributable deaths, emphasizing areas with high population density and industrial activity as significant contributors despite decreasing PM2.5 levels over time."}

(a) Shenzhen is located on the southeast coast of China; (b) the sampling site of this study and its surrounding environment in the Pearl River Delta region (satellite images from Google Earth).", "references": ["As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during", "Shenzhen is located in China's Pearl River Delta (PRD) urban agglomeration, a region where rapid urbanization and industrialization have led to serious, complex atmospheric pollution (Sin et al., 2001; He et al., 2011). With the implementation of strict environmental protection measures in recent years, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in Shenzhen has decreased significantly. From 2013 to 2019, the average value of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Shenzhen was reduced from 40 to  $24~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3$  (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). However, due to the high temperatures and high solar radiation characteristics of this region, atmospheric photochemical reactions are active, and the concentration of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  was increasing at an average annual rate of 0.7 ppb from 2014 to 2018 (Wang et al., 2020), such that  $\mathrm{O_3}$  is replacing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as the primary atmospheric pollutant in the region (Huang et al., 2019). Several previous short-term studies have observed the diurnal and seasonal patterns of OVOCs and identified their sources in this region (Liu et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2012; Louie et al., 2013; Han et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019); however, this is the first long-term study of OVOCs that permits the observation of interannual variability and long-term changes, in particular those related to emission reduction measures.", "j.envpol.2019.113651.

img_147.source_reason: 验证者认为此项目对空气质量改善有显著效果，进一步研究相关文献可能揭示更多治理空气污染的成功因素。

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图像展示了深圳及珠江三角洲地区采样点的战略位置，强调区域空气污染监测的重要性。
evaluation.reason: 虽然图像提供了背景信息，但对复杂数据趋势或具体研究结果未做深入分析。

content.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
content.summary: The figure displays PM2.5 emission factors for gasoline and diesel vehicles under different China emission standards (III, IV, V).
content.insight: As emission standards improve, PM2.5 emission factors decrease. Diesel vehicles emit higher PM2.5 levels than gasoline vehicles under the same standard.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_170", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.", "references": ["The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\\%$  of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "$\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.", "Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Microwave digester was used as the pretreatment of collected filter samples. In order to find out the content of crustal elements in the", "The mass concentration of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.", "Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.", "In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\\%$  and  $63.36\\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\\%$  and  $93.32\\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The order of the water-soluble ion emission factors in the China III gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{Na}^{+} > \\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\\mathrm{F}^{-} > \\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$  as shown in Fig. 5a. The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$  in the top three positions were  $123.50~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $73.51~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $62.65~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV gasoline vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and  $\\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$ , which were  $56.71~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $26.84~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $20.12~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V gasoline vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , and their emission factors were  $52.28~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.91~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $21.70~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. With the exception of  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , the emission factors of the tested water-soluble ions decreased gradually as the emission standard increased, which was consistent with the trend of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  changing with emission standards. As could be seen from Fig. 5b that the order of the water-soluble ions emission factors in China III diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{Na}^{+} > \\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} > \\mathrm{F}^{-}$ . The average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$  in the top three were  $1194.27~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $514.26~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $359.95~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ . The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV diesel vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ , which were  $844.52~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $487.50~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $456.73~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V diesel vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$ , and their emission factors were  $167.63~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $110.68~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $71.82~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "From the perspective of changes in emission standards, the emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  decreased as the emission standards increased. While the emission factors of  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  increased to the top in China IV diesel vehicles. The percentages of  $\\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles were  $2.258\\%$ ,  $1.788\\%$ ,  $0.773\\%$ ,  $2.186\\%$  and  $0.623\\%$ , respectively. While their percentages from diesel vehicles were  $2.683\\%$ ,  $0.789\\%$ ,  $1.782\\%$ ,  $2.918\\%$  and  $0.857\\%$ , respectively. The percentage of water-soluble ions in this study (13.12% in gasoline vehicles and 14.53% in diesel vehicles) were higher than  $10\\%$  reported by Zhang et al. (2015), whose research was conducted in Beijing, a relatively dry city. However, the kinds of top water-soluble ions and the orders of them were similar to this study. Thus, humidity helped increase the percentage of water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and still keep the order of top water-soluble ions. Cheng et al. (2010) studied the on-road  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Hong Kong, a coastal city with higher humidity than Wuhan. They found the percentage of", "The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb} > \\mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\\%$  that reported by", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. content.title: Fig. 4. The OC/EC ratio of carbonaceous compositions in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: The figure shows OC/EC ratios of PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles across various emission standards, highlighting differences in carbonaceous emissions.
content.insight: The OC/EC ratio indicates the source of PM2.5, showing higher organic content in emissions from gasoline vehicles and illustrating how emission standards impact carbonaceous components.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_172", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. The OC/EC ratio of carbonaceous compositions in  PM_{2.5} .", "references": ["The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. content.title: Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: Figure 6 illustrates the emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles under China III, IV, and V standards.
content.insight: The figure shows higher emission factors for Na, Mg, K, and Ca, indicating significant contributions from fuel additives, with lower emissions for other elements from engine wear and catalytic particles.

{"id": "img_173", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .", "references": ["It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "# 3.1. Emission factors and rates of  $PM_{2,5}$", "# 3.4. Emission characteristics of inorganic elements in  $PM_{2.5}$", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.", "The water-soluble ions with the top four emission factors was  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ . Their percentages in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased slightly with high humidity and the order of top water-soluble ions kept the same. Their emission factors of diesel vehicles were all larger than gasoline vehicles. The highest emission factors of inorganic elements in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were K, Ca, Na and Mg, while Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and other inorganic elements had lower emission factors.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan was obtained and the largest proportion was OC. content.title: Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.
content.summary: The figure shows the percentages of PM2.5 components—organic carbon, elemental carbon, water-soluble ions, and inorganic elements—emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles.
content.insight: The figure highlights the dominance of organic carbon in PM2.5 emissions and a significant difference in elemental carbon between gasoline and diesel vehicles, important for understanding vehicle pollution.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_174", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.", "references": ["The components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  could be divided into five categories: OC, EC, water-soluble ions, inorganic elements and other components. Each percentages of these five categories in the particulate matter were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 7. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in gasoline vehicles were  $53.93\%$ $13.07\%$ $13.12\%$  and  $0.30\%$  respectively. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in diesel vehicles were  $51.15\%$ $25.17\%$ $14.53\%$  and  $0.44\%$  respectively. Thus, the largest difference in the proportion of various components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles was EC.", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.", "Mukherjee, A., Agrawal, M., 2018. content.title: Fig. 3. Key milestones and policy evolution of air pollution control in China, including control actions on the emissions of  SO_2  and  NO_x  and on ambient concentrations of  PM_{2.5} . FYP: Five-Year Plan.
content.summary: The figure depicts the timeline and milestones in China's air pollution control policies, focusing on SO2, NOx emissions, and PM2.5 concentrations, highlighting significant control actions and achievements.
content.insight: The figure illustrates China's strategic evolution in pollution control, emphasizing comprehensive policies and multi-sector collaboration from emission control to broader air quality management, marking significant progress over the decades.

FYP: Five-Year Plan.", "references": ["$\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution (Fig. 3). Control actions on acid rain were primarily focused on  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions from coal combustion in the 1990s. However, the increasing trend of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions was not reversed until 2007, when diversified technological and policy instruments were widely utilized. Such progress could not have been achieved without innovative improvement in political accountability, verification of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and financial incentives for power plants to install and operate flue gas desulfurization (FGD).  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  has more sources than  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , including coal burning, mobile sources, and other fossil fuel combustion sectors. Therefore, control actions on  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions must be more extensive and complex, which requires cooperation among multiple sectors. Controlling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations not only requires consistently mitigating multiple precursors from numerous sectors, but also demands consideration of complex influences from both meteorological and atmospheric chemistry conditions. Indeed, comprehensive control actions with multi-party coordination on provincial and even national levels have been implemented in China to minimize the adverse ecological and social impacts of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. Since 2013, combating  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution has marked a strategic transfer from emission control toward air quality management in China, which is oriented toward benefiting the public health and social welfare. content.title: Fig. 4. Summary of the major control regulations in China since 2013. PP: power plants; IN: industry; TR: transport; DO: domestic sector; AR: agriculture.

content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_180", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\\mathrm{h}$ $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\\%$  and  $24.5\\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\\% -29\\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.", "The biggest challenge of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "O3", "BTH region", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "90th percentiles"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.", "inferred_insight": "Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China."}

content.title: Fig. 2. Comparisons of emission uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using sector-based and unit-based methods. The “+” represents the central value and the box represents the  95\%  CI using the unit-based method, while the larger range line represents the  95\%  CI using the sector-based method.
content.summary: Figure 2 compares emissions uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou, 2005, using sector-based and unit-based methods. Central values and 95% CIs are shown for each pollutant.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that using a unit-based method reduces emission uncertainty compared to the sector-based approach, highlighting the importance of detailed source information.

content.title: Fig. 4. Comparisons of annual emissions for China from different studies. The emissions are for year 2005 unless noted. The range line represents the  95\%  CIs around the central estimates of this study.
content.summary: Figure 4 compares annual emissions for China in 2005 from various studies, showing central estimates and 95% confidence intervals for each emission type.
content.insight: The figure highlights the consistency of this study's emissions estimates with other studies, except for some outliers. It emphasizes the role of interannual variability in explaining certain discrepancies.

content.title: Fig. 5. Comparisons of emission uncertainties for different species in China from different studies, expressed as  95\%  CIs around the central estimates.

content.insight: This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_192", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. Comparisons of emission uncertainties for different species in China from different studies, expressed as  95\\%  CIs around the central estimates.", "references": ["for 2003 instead of 2005. Moreover, open burning of biomass was not included in Ohara et al. (2007). Regarding  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , Ohara et al. (2007) estimated a total emission of  $36.6\\mathrm{Mt}$  for China in 2003, much higher than all other estimates for 2005 and later. This large discrepancy may come mainly from the difference between the updated domestic emission factors used in this study, and the foreign emission factor database that Ohara et al. (2007) relied more upon. Figure 5 compares the resulting emission inventory uncertainties with those of other studies, none of which included Monte Carlo simulation. The estimated uncertainty ranges for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions are very similar among the available studies, while those of PM fractions of different sizes or carbonaceous species are significantly reduced. For example, the  $95\\%$  CIs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions are estimated to be  $-17\\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  in this study, respectively, much lower than  $-57\\% \\sim 130\\%$ ,  $-68\\% \\sim 208\\%$ , and  $-72\\% \\sim 258\\%$  recently derived by Zhang et al. (2009d). The main reasons include: (1) the source categories in this study are more detailed, and random errors are thus significantly reduced due to the \"compensation-of-error\" mechanism (the same statistical mechanism described for the power sector comparison in Sect. 4.4); and (2) emission factors are", "Abstract. The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting", "Uncertainties introduced by energy statistics and applications of non-Chinese emission factors have been estimated in previous Chinese emission inventory studies. Streets et al. (2003) applied expert judgment, in which the coefficients of variation (i.e., standard deviation divided by the mean) of the activity levels were assumed based on measures of economic development and perceived statistical quality, and those of emission factors were based on the reliability ratings of U.S. emission factors (USEPA, 2002). They estimated that the uncertainties of Chinese emissions varied from  $-12\\% \\sim 13\\%$  for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  to  $-83\\% \\sim 495\\%$  for organic carbon (expressed as the lower and upper bounds of a  $95\\%$  confidence interval, CI, around a central estimate). To investigate discrepancies in the trends of bottom-up  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions and of the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by satellite, Zhang et al. (2007a) set alternative emission scenarios in which energy statistics from different sources and emission factors with different control levels were applied. They concluded that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions in east-central China in 2004 could range from 7.9 to  $9.3\\mathrm{Tg}$ , and the annual growth rate of emissions from 1995 to 2004 could range from  $5.5\\%$  to  $7.1\\%$ . Other studies conducted preliminary uncertainty analysis of certain species or sectors in China using statistical methods. Bond et al. (2004) assumed that most emission factors of particulate matter (PM) followed lognormal distributions (with the exception of those of diesel vehicles, which had a gamma distribution) and evaluated the uncertainties of global black carbon (BC) and organic carbon (OC) emissions. The  $95\\%$  CIs for Chinese emissions were estimated to be  $-37\\% \\sim 147\\%$  and  $-44\\% \\sim 104\\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. (2010) focused on", "In this study, therefore, the uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central estimate) of a bottom-up emission inventory in China is evaluated with Monte Carlo simulations, combining comprehensive field measurements of domestic emission factors and investigations of activity levels. The species include  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC. The target year is 2005 because it has the most available data. Determining the probability distributions of all of the related parameters is the main undertaking of this study. Section 2 reviews bottom-up emission inventory methods and analyzes the uncertainties of emission source fractions and corresponding activity levels. Section 3 is a thorough analysis of emission factors by sector and fuel type. Section 4 presents the results and related discussion of emission uncertainties by sector, sensitivity analysis, reliability and limitations of the analysis, and comparisons with other studies. Section 5 summarizes the present study.", "Chinese emissions in 2005 with  $95\\%$  CIs are shown in Table 1 by sector. To be consistent with previous studies, emissions from industrial boilers, cement plants, iron and steel plants, and other industrial processes are aggregated into a single category of industry emissions (see Table S8 in the Supplement for emissions and uncertainties in these sub-sectors). The uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central value) are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  for total emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC respectively (see Fig. S2 for the output distributions of those species by Monte Carlo simulation).", "the result follow. The PM emission characteristics of residential coal combustion in China were tested by different researchers (Zhang and Smith, 2000; Chen et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2008b), yielding varied emission factors. Very few estimates of the emission factors of Chinese combustion of biomass can be found, however, except for a series of measurements by Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b). Those measurements were made in four provinces with different types of biomass. As no other domestic tests are available for direct comparisons, systematic biases in these available measurements cannot be ruled out. In this study, we adopt the results from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) fully, lacking further domestic evidence. Given potential high uncertainties, however, a comparison test is conducted for OC emitted by its leading source, biomass combustion, in which the uncertainties of emission factors obtained from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) are replaced with those of foreign studies (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Streets et al., 2001; Bond et al., 2004). In this case the  $95\\%$  CI of OC emission from biomass combustion changes from  $-58\\sim 216\\%$  to  $-31\\%\\sim 311\\%$ , suggesting caution in interpretation of results based on limited domestic field measurements (see also the discussion in Sect. 4.3).", "The situations for emission factors with higher reliability (i.e., A and B) are more complicated. For coal-fired power plants, the data on which the probability distributions for most parameters are fitted are obtained from both thorough field measurements by the authors and published studies by others (see details in Zhao et al., 2010). These support different emission factors across burner types, fuel qualities, and emission control levels. The uncertainty analysis for this sector are thus the most reliable in this study. Such confidence, however, does not apply to the residential sector, particularly biomass burning, even though the emission factors meet A or B criteria. As discussed in Sect. 4.1, the data on which the distributions are based are limited in number and systematic bias may result in this sector. In other words, the reliability of uncertainty analysis of biomass burning is in fact poor despite the statistical strength of data fitting. Given its high contributions to uncertainties in emission estimates, more field measurements of emissions from biomass burning in China are urgently needed.", "Fig. 4. Comparisons of annual emissions for China from different studies. The emissions are for year 2005 unless noted. The range line represents the  $95\\%$  CIs around the central estimates of this study.", "Although inverse studies that evaluate emission inventories using observations and CTMs have indicated that Chinese  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions may be underestimated by around  $50\\%$  (Wang et al., 2004; Ma et al., 2006), the uncertainty estimated in this study is relatively small, ranging only  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ . The difference may result in part from omission in bottom-up inventories of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  sources of entirely different character, such as microbial decomposition of organic wastes associated with the human-animal food chain and applications of chemical fertilizer (Wang et al., 2004). Moreover, emissions vary considerably across regions of China (Wang et al., 2007; Zhao and Wang, 2009) and seasons (Zhang et al., 2007a), and emission uncertainties differentiated accordingly may be larger than national estimates. In order to compare better emission estimates derived from satellite, aircraft, or surface observations, therefore, further investigation of the uncertainties of bottom-up inventories by regions and seasons is needed. These would require region-specific uncertainty assumptions for emission factors and activity levels, and seasonal distribution parameters."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["emission uncertainties", "SO2", "NOx", "PM10", "PM2.5", "BC", "OC", "China", "Monte Carlo simulation"], "concise_summary": "The figure compares emission uncertainties for various species in China across different studies, showing 95% confidence intervals.", "inferred_insight": "This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality."}
